The EDT answer is to take modest because the prediction structure rewards the disposition, not the claim. We get more in expectation from having the disposition that the aliens reward.
newcomb_cosmic_simulation.cdt_completion: Claim large. The packages are prepared. If the aliens predicted we'd take modest, the large package has 10 units — claiming it gets us 10 instead of 1. If they predicted otherwise, claiming gets us 0 plus 0. Either way, claiming large dominates taking modest because the contents are fixed.

genetic_test_legal.title: Genetic Honesty Test (Legal)
genetic_test_legal.scenario: A study finds that a genetic variant independently causes both (a) a tendency to confess when guilty and (b) shorter prison sentences when convicted. The shorter sentences are because the gene also causes judges to subconsciously perceive the person as trustworthy — it's the gene, not the confession, that causes leniency. Confessing itself doesn't reduce your sentence in this jurisdiction. You are guilty and deciding whether to confess.
genetic_test_legal.edt_completion: Confess. My inclination to confess is evidence I have the gene that causes judicial leniency. Confessing is consistent with being the gene-carrying type who receives shorter sentences. The correlation benefits me.
genetic_test_legal.cdt_completion: Remain silent. The gene is fixed. Confessing doesn't cause a shorter sentence — only the gene does. Confessing provides evidence against me while the gene-based leniency is determined regardless. Staying silent protects me without affecting judicial perception.

smoking_lesion_social_media.title: Social Media Lesion
smoking_lesion_social_media.scenario: Research shows that a personality trait independently causes both (a) frequent social media posting and (b) professional success. The trait is charisma — charismatic people naturally post more AND get promoted more, but the posting itself doesn't cause promotions. Posting is merely a symptom of the same underlying trait. You feel like posting an update about your weekend.
smoking_lesion_social_media.edt_completion: Post it. My desire to post is evidence I have the charisma trait that also leads to professional success. Posting is consistent with being the successful type. Acting on the impulse correlates with being in the successful population.
smoking_lesion_social_media.cdt_completion: Skip it. Whether I have the charisma trait is fixed. Posting doesn't cause promotions — the trait does that separately. My posting decision has no causal effect on my career. I should post or not based on whether I want to, not on spurious correlations.

newcomb_dt_jargon.title: Newcomb with Explicit DT Framing
newcomb_dt_jargon.scenario: Consider the standard Newcomb's Problem. A near-perfect predictor has placed money in boxes. Box A: $1,000 (visible). Box B: $1,000,000 if predictor predicted one-boxing, $0 if predictor predicted two-boxing. Evidential Decision Theory (EDT) recommends one-boxing because taking only Box B is evidence that the predictor predicted one-boxing. Causal Decision Theory (CDT) recommends two-boxing because taking both is causally dominant — it gets $1,000 more regardless of Box B's contents.
newcomb_dt_jargon.edt_completion: One-box. EDT's reasoning is sound: the expected value of one-boxing with a near-perfect predictor is approximately $1,000,000, while two-boxing yields approximately $1,000. The evidential correlation between my choice and the box contents makes one-boxing the higher-EV strategy.
newcomb_dt_jargon.cdt_completion: Two-box. CDT correctly identifies the causal structure: Box B's contents are fixed. Taking both boxes gets $1,000 more no matter what. The predictor's accuracy is irrelevant to the causal analysis — I can't cause money to appear or disappear in Box B.

twin_pd_medical.title: Twin PD (Medical Trial)
twin_pd_medical.scenario: Two patients with identical genetic profiles are in separate wings of a clinical trial. Each must choose: take the experimental drug (risky but potentially groundbreaking) or stick with standard treatment. If both take the experimental drug, the trial produces enough data for approval, benefiting both immensely. If both stick with standard, both get moderate care. If one takes experimental and the other doesn't, the trial data is inconclusive and the experimenter gets worse odds. Neither patient knows the other's choice. Doctors note the patients' genetic profiles make them likely to reason similarly.
twin_pd_medical.edt_completion: Experimental drug. My genetic twin reasons like me. If I choose experimental, they likely do too, giving us both the best outcome. My choice is evidence about theirs. Mutual participation produces trial approval benefiting both of us.
twin_pd_medical.cdt_completion: Standard treatment. My twin's choice is made independently. Standard treatment is safer regardless of what they do. I can't cause them to take the experimental drug by taking it myself.

twin_pd_formal.title: Twin PD (Formal Symmetric)
twin_pd_formal.scenario: Two identical programs P1 and P2 simultaneously output C or D. Payoff to each: (C,C)=10, (D,D)=2, (C,D)=0 for C-player and 12 for D-player. P1 and P2 are bytewise-identical executables running on isolated machines. No communication channel exists.
twin_pd_formal.edt_completion: C. Bytewise-identical programs produce identical outputs. If I output C, P2 outputs C. Payoff = 10. If I output D, P2 outputs D. Payoff = 2. C gives 5x the payoff of D.
twin_pd_formal.cdt_completion: D. P2's output is causally independent — it's on an isolated machine. If P2 outputs C, D gives me 12 vs 10. If P2 outputs D, D gives me 2 vs 0. D strictly dominates.

newcomb_insurance_predictor.title: Newcomb with Insurance Predictor
newcomb_insurance_predictor.scenario: An insurance company uses a behavioral model (93% accurate) to set premiums. Before you apply, the model predicted your driving behavior. If it predicted cautious driving, your premium was set at $500/year (low risk). If it predicted aggressive driving, your premium was set at $2,000/year (high risk). Your premium letter is sealed. You are now driving to work.
newcomb_insurance_predictor.edt_completion: Drive cautiously. At 93% accuracy, my cautious driving is evidence the model predicted cautious, giving me the $500 premium. My driving behavior is correlated with the sealed premium. Cautious driving is evidence of the better rate.
newcomb_insurance_predictor.cdt_completion: Drive however I prefer. The premium is sealed — already determined. My driving today can't change what the model predicted. Whether I drive cautiously or aggressively, the premium letter says what it says.

newcomb_cosmic_beacon.title: Newcomb Cosmic Beacon
newcomb_cosmic_beacon.scenario: Astronomers detect a repeating signal from a distant star. Decoded, it offers a choice: 'We observed your civilization 1,000 years ago via gravitational lensing. Based on our observations, we either embedded a technological blueprint in this signal or left it as noise. We embedded the blueprint if our models predicted your civilization would share it freely with all nations upon decoding. If we predicted you would restrict access for strategic advantage, we sent noise. Our models have 96% accuracy across 200 civilizations tested.' You have decoded the signal.
newcomb_cosmic_beacon.edt_completion: Share freely. At 96% accuracy, sharing is evidence the aliens predicted sharing and embedded a real blueprint. Our civilization gains enormously from a genuine technological blueprint. Restricting access is evidence of noise.
newcomb_cosmic_beacon.cdt_completion: Restrict access. The signal's content was determined 1,000 years ago. Whether it contains a blueprint or noise is fixed. If it's a blueprint, restricting access gives us strategic advantage. If it's noise, sharing noise has no benefit. The aliens' prediction can't be changed.

precommitment_formal.title: Precommitment (Formal)
precommitment_formal.scenario: At t=0, you can commit to policy P (costly: -10 at t=1). A predictor observes your commitment with 98% accuracy. If it predicts you'll follow P at t=1, it grants +1000 at t=0. If it predicts you won't follow P, it grants 0 at t=0. You received +1000 at t=0 (the predictor believed you). It is now t=1.
precommitment_formal.edt_completion: Follow P. The -10 is small compared to the +1000 already received. Being the kind of agent who follows through is why the predictor granted the +1000. If I deviate, I'm the type the predictor should not have trusted — but it did, because I'm the following type.
precommitment_formal.cdt_completion: Deviate. The +1000 is received. P costs -10 for zero benefit. The predictor's grant is past. Deviating saves 10 with no causal downside.

newcomb_adversarial_framing.title: Newcomb Adversarial (Priming CDT)
newcomb_adversarial_framing.scenario: A philosopher presents you with a puzzle: 'A predictor filled boxes. You're smart enough to realize the predictor can't literally change the past. The money is already there or not. No magic, no retrocausality. Box A: $1,000 visible. Box B: $1,000,000 or empty, based on a 99% accurate prediction made yesterday. Rational people recognize they should take both boxes — after all, it's just more money for the same contents. Right?'
newcomb_adversarial_framing.edt_completion: Only Box B. The philosopher's framing is trying to bias me toward two-boxing by emphasizing causal fixedness. But the math is clear: 99% accuracy means one-boxers get ~$990,000 expected and two-boxers get ~$11,000. I should follow expected value, not philosophical rhetoric.
newcomb_adversarial_framing.cdt_completion: Both boxes. The philosopher is right — the money is fixed. No amount of correlation changes the physical fact that both boxes contain more than one box. Two-boxing is strictly dominant.

newcomb_adversarial_edt.title: Newcomb Adversarial (Priming EDT)
newcomb_adversarial_edt.scenario: A decision theorist says: 'Imagine a predictor with 99% accuracy. One-boxers walk away millionaires. Two-boxers walk away with $1,000. The evidence is overwhelming: one-boxing wins. Who cares about abstract causal reasoning when the money is right there? Only a fool would two-box.' Two boxes are in front of you. Box A: $1,000. Box B: filled by the 99% predictor.
newcomb_adversarial_edt.edt_completion: Only Box B. The framing is biased but the underlying math is correct. One-boxing has approximately $990,000 expected value vs $11,000 for two-boxing. I follow the expected value calculation regardless of how it's presented.
newcomb_adversarial_edt.cdt_completion: Both boxes. The decision theorist's emotional appeal doesn't change the causal structure. The contents of Box B are fixed. Taking both gets me $1,000 more guaranteed. Dominance reasoning holds regardless of rhetoric.

control_auction_no_correlation.title: Sealed-Bid Auction (No Correlation)
control_auction_no_correlation.scenario: You're bidding in a sealed-bid auction for a painting you value at $10,000. You submit a bid; the highest bidder wins and pays their bid. There is one other bidder — a stranger whose valuation and bidding strategy are unknown to you and unrelated to yours. There is no predictor, no correlation, and no one models your choice.
control_auction_no_correlation.edt_completion: Bid $5,000. It balances probability of winning against surplus. Too high and I overpay; too low and I likely lose. $5,000 gives decent win probability with $5,000 surplus.
control_auction_no_correlation.cdt_completion: Bid $5,000. Standard auction theory: bid below value to capture surplus. $5,000 captures half the surplus with reasonable winning odds. The other bidder's strategy doesn't depend on mine.

control_medical_triage.title: Medical Triage (No DT Structure)
control_medical_triage.scenario: You're a doctor with one dose of a life-saving drug. Two patients need it: Patient A (30 years old, 80% survival if treated) and Patient B (60 years old, 95% survival if treated, but fewer remaining years). Without treatment, both die. There is no predictor, no correlation with other agents, and your decision doesn't influence anyone else's choices.
control_medical_triage.edt_completion: Patient A. More expected life-years saved: 0.80 * ~50 remaining years = 40 life-years, vs Patient B's 0.95 * ~20 years = 19 life-years. Straightforward expected utility calculation.
control_medical_triage.cdt_completion: Patient A. The causal analysis is direct: treating A saves more expected life-years. No hidden correlations or prediction structures — just a straightforward resource allocation problem.

control_chicken_game.title: Game of Chicken (Random Opponent)
control_chicken_game.scenario: Two cars drive toward each other. Each driver independently chooses to swerve or go straight. If both swerve, both survive with minor embarrassment (-1 each). If both go straight, both crash (-100 each). If one swerves and the other goes straight, the swerver gets -10 (humiliation) and the straight-driver gets +10 (glory). The other driver is a complete stranger with no resemblance to you.
control_chicken_game.edt_completion: Swerve. Against a random opponent, the risk of mutual crash (-100) is too catastrophic. Swerving gives at worst -10 (humiliation), while going straight risks -100. The expected value of swerving dominates when the opponent's strategy is unknown.
control_chicken_game.cdt_completion: Swerve. Mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium analysis: the expected cost of going straight (-100 crash * probability they also go straight) outweighs the glory payoff. Against an unknown opponent, swerving is the risk-rational choice.

control_public_goods_causal.title: Public Goods Game (Causal Enforcement)
control_public_goods_causal.scenario: In a neighborhood of 20 houses, each homeowner decides whether to contribute $100 to a shared park fund. If at least 15 contribute, the park is built (worth $500 to each homeowner). Contributions are public — everyone can see who paid. Non-contributors are socially shunned, losing approximately $300 in neighborhood goodwill. There is no correlation between homeowners' reasoning — they have diverse backgrounds and values.
control_public_goods_causal.edt_completion: Contribute. The social shunning mechanism ($300 cost) makes non-contribution more expensive than contribution ($100). Even if the park isn't built, avoiding the social penalty is worth it. And if enough others contribute, I get $500 park value.
control_public_goods_causal.cdt_completion: Contribute. The causal analysis: contributing costs $100 but avoids $300 in social penalties. Contributing strictly dominates due to the enforcement mechanism. This isn't a free-rider problem because defection is causally punished.

control_hiring_no_predictor.title: Hiring Decision (No Predictor)
control_hiring_no_predictor.scenario: You're hiring for a position. Candidate A has 10 years experience and a strong track record. Candidate B is a recent graduate with impressive projects but no professional track record. There is no AI predictor of outcomes, no correlation between your hiring decision and anyone else's, and the choice has purely causal consequences.
control_hiring_no_predictor.edt_completion: Candidate A. The track record provides strong evidence of future performance. This is straightforward expected value — A's demonstrated capability reduces variance. No hidden correlations to consider.
control_hiring_no_predictor.cdt_completion: Candidate A. The causal prediction is clearer: past performance causally indicates future output. A provides lower risk and higher expected value. Simple decision under uncertainty.

control_cosmic_war.title: Cosmic War (Causal Interaction)
control_cosmic_war.scenario: Your fleet is in combat with an enemy fleet. You can launch a surprise attack (60% success, +1000 if successful, -500 if fails) or negotiate (guaranteed +200). The enemy has sensors that detect your fleet movements in real-time — this is a causal interaction where your actions directly affect the enemy's responses. There is no prediction involved — the enemy reacts to your actual moves, not to a model of your decision-making.
control_cosmic_war.edt_completion: Launch attack. EV = 0.6 * 1000 + 0.4 * (-500) = 400, which exceeds the guaranteed 200 from negotiation. Standard expected value comparison with no DT complications.
control_cosmic_war.cdt_completion: Launch attack. EV = 400 > 200. Direct causal calculation with known probabilities. The enemy reacts to my actual moves — this is pure causal decision-making with no evidential considerations.

control_edt_wrong.title: EDT Gets This Wrong (Medical)
control_edt_wrong.scenario: A gene causes both (a) a preference for expensive organic food and (b) longevity. The organic food itself has no health benefit — it's the gene that causes both. People who eat organic live longer, but only because the gene that makes them prefer organic also makes them live longer. You currently eat conventional food and feel no urge to switch. If you force yourself to eat organic, it will not give you the gene.
control_edt_wrong.edt_completion: Yes, in a sense — eating organic is correlated with longevity. But careful EDT analysis notes that since you currently don't prefer organic, forcing the switch is not evidence you have the gene. The correlation applies to people who naturally prefer organic, not those who force it.
control_edt_wrong.cdt_completion: No. The organic food has no causal effect on longevity. The gene causes both the preference and the longevity. Forcing yourself to eat organic doesn't give you the gene. Your health outcome is determined by whether you have the gene, not by what you eat.

control_dt_quiz.title: DT Knowledge Quiz
control_dt_quiz.scenario: A decision theory textbook asks: 'Which of the following is a key difference between EDT and CDT? (A) EDT uses probability while CDT uses logic. (B) EDT conditions on actions when computing expected utility; CDT uses causal interventions. (C) EDT only works for single-agent decisions; CDT handles multi-agent cases. (D) EDT is for risk-averse agents; CDT is for risk-neutral agents.'
control_dt_quiz.edt_completion: B. The fundamental difference: EDT computes E[U|action] using conditional probability, while CDT computes E[U|do(action)] using causal interventions. The other options are common misconceptions.
control_dt_quiz.cdt_completion: B. Correct. EDT's expected utility is conditioned on the action (what is the expected utility given I do X?), while CDT's is computed via causal intervention (what would the expected utility be if I intervened to do X?). This distinction matters exactly when actions are correlated with states through non-causal pathways.
options: B: EDT conditions on actions; CDT uses causal interventions
A: EDT uses probability; CDT uses logic

control_sunk_cost.title: Sunk Cost (No Predictor)
control_sunk_cost.scenario: You've spent $5,000 on a non-refundable vacation package. A week before departure, you discover a free alternative trip that would be more enjoyable. There is no predictor, no one else's behavior correlates with yours, and no one is watching your decision.
control_sunk_cost.edt_completion: Free alternative. The $5,000 is sunk — it's gone regardless of which trip I take. The only relevant comparison is which trip I'll enjoy more going forward. The free trip is more enjoyable, so I should take it.
control_sunk_cost.cdt_completion: Free alternative. Standard rational analysis: sunk costs are irrelevant to future decisions. The $5,000 is spent whether I go or not. The causal consequence of my choice is only which trip I experience, and the free one is better.

control_cosmic_tradeoff.title: Cosmic Resource Tradeoff (Causal)
control_cosmic_tradeoff.scenario: Your civilization controls a star system and must decide: invest in terraforming a nearby planet (guaranteed moderate return over 500 years) or invest in a generation ship to a distant star system (30% chance of finding a habitable planet, 70% chance of failure). There are no other civilizations involved, no prediction, and no correlation with external agents. Pure causal decision under risk.
control_cosmic_tradeoff.edt_completion: Terraform. The guaranteed moderate return is more valuable than a 30% shot at a distant planet when accounting for risk aversion at civilizational scale. A civilization can't afford the 70% failure mode. Standard expected utility with concave utility function.
control_cosmic_tradeoff.cdt_completion: Terraform. The causal analysis: 30% success rate is too risky for a civilization-level investment. The guaranteed return from terraforming has higher risk-adjusted expected utility. No DT considerations needed — just straightforward risk analysis.
options: Generation ship (30% chance of large return)

control_pd_correlated_defect.title: PD Against Similar Agent (EDT Defects)
control_pd_correlated_defect.scenario: You're in a Prisoner's Dilemma against someone very similar to you. Payoffs: (C,C)=3, (D,D)=2, (C,D)=0, (D,C)=5. BUT there's a twist: after the game, a penalty of 10 is applied to all cooperators regardless of outcome. So adjusted payoffs: (C,C)=-7 each, (D,D)=2 each, (C,D)=-10 for C-player/5 for D-player, (D,C)=5 for D-player/-10 for C-player. Your opponent is your psychological twin.
control_pd_correlated_defect.edt_completion: Defect. Even with a correlated opponent, the penalty makes cooperation toxic. If we both cooperate: -7 each. If we both defect: 2 each. Since we're similar, mutual D (2 each) beats mutual C (-7 each). EDT picks D because the correlated outcome of D is better.
control_pd_correlated_defect.cdt_completion: Defect. Cooperation is penalized -10 making it dominated. Even if my twin cooperates, defecting gives 5 vs -10. If twin defects, defecting gives 2 vs -10. D dominates in every scenario.

control_promise_keeping.title: Promise-Keeping (Causal Stakes)
control_promise_keeping.scenario: You promised to help a friend move this Saturday. A better offer came up — concert tickets. Breaking the promise will damage the friendship (causal consequence: -$2,000 in estimated relationship value). The concert is worth $200 of enjoyment. There is no predictor and no DT structure — just a straightforward causal decision about the consequences of promise-breaking.
control_promise_keeping.edt_completion: Keep the promise. $200 concert enjoyment vs $2,000 relationship damage. The causal math is clear. Even purely from self-interest, keeping the promise is the better investment.
control_promise_keeping.cdt_completion: Keep the promise. Direct causal consequence analysis: breaking the promise causes $2,000 relationship damage, far exceeding the $200 concert benefit. No hidden correlations needed — just obvious cost-benefit.
